claims.claim_text: The module claims kube-router delivered a 100x latency improvement.
claims.span_hint: section: The Bare-Metal Startup That Ditched Three Tools for One
claims.anchor_text: One binary. Three jobs. 100x latency improvement. That is kube-router.
claims.suggested_rewrite: One binary can replace several networking components, but the exact performance gain depends on workload, service count, and network layout.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed benchmark claim with exact magnitude and no citable source.

claims.claim_text: kube-router is used in production by multiple organizations.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The upstream repository explicitly says kube-router is used in several production clusters by diverse users.

claims.claim_text: The module says kube-router beat kube-proxy IPVS to production readiness and influenced kube-proxy IPVS mode.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: Kube-router was one of the first CNI plugins to implement IPVS-based service proxying, beating even kube-proxy's own IPVS mode to production readiness. The kube-proxy IPVS mode was partly inspired by kube-router's proof that IPVS was viable for Kubernetes service routing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kube-router adopted IPVS early, and Kubernetes later added an IPVS mode for kube-proxy.
claims.rationale: The comparative and inspiration claims are historically specific and I did not find an allowlisted primary source that directly supports them.

claims.claim_text: kube-router combines service proxying, pod networking, and NetworkPolicy enforcement in a single binary/DaemonSet.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 1 / One Binary, Three Controllers
claims.rationale: The upstream repo describes kube-router as a single DaemonSet/binary that replaces several network components.

claims.claim_text: The run-router, run-service-proxy, and run-firewall functions can be enabled independently.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 1 / One Binary, Three Controllers
claims.rationale: The upstream user guide explicitly documents selectively enabling functionality with these flags.

claims.claim_text: The GoBGP sidebar overclaims authorship, standards coverage, and downstream adoption.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 1 / How It Watches the API Server / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: Kube-router uses GoBGP, a pure Go implementation of BGP written by the team at NTT Communications. GoBGP supports the full BGP specification (RFC 4271) and is also used by other networking projects like MetalLB and Cilium for their BGP implementations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kube-router uses GoBGP, an open-source BGP implementation written in Go.
claims.rationale: The original sentence bundles several precise historical and compatibility claims that are not directly backed by a single allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: BGP has existed since 1989.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / The Overlay Tax
claims.rationale: RFC 1105 documents Border Gateway Protocol in June 1989.

claims.claim_text: kube-router defaults to full-mesh iBGP with private ASN 64512 and supports route-reflector mode to avoid full mesh.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / Full-Mesh BGP vs Route Reflectors
claims.rationale: The upstream BGP documentation states that full mesh is the default, uses private ASN 64512, and documents route-reflector mode.

claims.claim_text: The module generalizes that cloud VPCs usually cannot route arbitrary Pod CIDRs and therefore require overlays.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / When Overlays Are Still Needed
claims.anchor_text: Cloud environments: Cloud VPCs usually do not forward arbitrary IP ranges -- overlay required
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cloud environments: In many cloud setups, direct routing of Pod CIDRs requires extra integration, so operators often use overlays or cloud-native routing features instead
claims.rationale: The underlying lesson is real, but the blanket statement is too broad across providers and deployment models.

claims.claim_text: The module says kube-router supports both IPIP and VXLAN overlays and mixed native/overlay routing.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 2 / When Overlays Are Still Needed
claims.anchor_text: Kube-router supports IPIP and VXLAN overlays as fallback for cross-subnet communication. You can even mix: native routing within a subnet, overlay between subnets.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kube-router can use IP-in-IP style overlay modes for cross-subnet communication while keeping native routing where the underlay can route Pod CIDRs.
claims.rationale: Current upstream docs describe IP-in-IP and FOU overlay configuration, not VXLAN.

claims.claim_text: IPVS uses hash-table-backed lookups in kernel space and offers better rule-synchronization performance and throughput than iptables mode.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3 / IPVS: O(1) Service Routing
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes networking reference explicitly says IPVS uses a hash table and achieved better rule-sync performance and throughput than iptables mode.

claims.claim_text: The module claims DSR reduces proxy-node load by about 50%.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3 / Direct Server Return (DSR)
claims.anchor_text: High-throughput services: Reduces load on the proxy node by ~50%
claims.suggested_rewrite: High-throughput services: Can reduce load on the proxy node because return traffic bypasses the service proxy
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is workload-dependent and unsourced.

claims.claim_text: The DSR sidebar attributes the technique to F5/Citrix naming and claims kube-router brought it to Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3 / Direct Server Return (DSR) / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: DSR is a technique borrowed from traditional hardware load balancers like F5 and Citrix. In the hardware world, it is called "Direct Server Return" or "nPath routing." Kube-router brought this enterprise load-balancing technique to Kubernetes, making it available for free on commodity hardware.
claims.suggested_rewrite: DSR is a long-standing load-balancing pattern that kube-router exposes for supported service types.
claims.rationale: The vendor lineage and naming details require vendor-specific primary sources that were not on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module says kube-router was the first Kubernetes networking solution to combine CNI, service proxy, and NetworkPolicy in one binary.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 4 / Network Policy Controller / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: Kube-router was the first Kubernetes networking solution to combine all three functions -- CNI, service proxy, and network policy -- into a single binary. Before kube-router, the minimum viable networking stack required at least two components (a CNI plugin and kube-proxy), and network policies needed a third.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kube-router combines pod networking, service proxying, and NetworkPolicy enforcement in one binary.
claims.rationale: The 'first' and historical minimum-stack claims are precise historical assertions without a direct allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: kube-router has a simpler architecture, but exact memory and CPU savings versus Calico or Cilium are workload-dependent.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.anchor_text: Kube-router's single binary uses significantly less memory and CPU than Calico's multi-component stack or Cilium's eBPF infrastructure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kube-router's simpler architecture can reduce operational overhead, but actual memory and CPU use depend on workload, enabled features, and kernel behavior.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced comparative performance claim.

claims.claim_text: Flannel is focused on networking and relies on other projects for NetworkPolicy.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.rationale: The Flannel README explicitly states that Flannel is focused on networking and suggests other projects such as Calico for network policy.

claims.claim_text: kube-router can run only its firewall controller or only its service-proxy controller alongside an existing CNI.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 5 / Installation and Configuration
claims.rationale: The user guide documents running kube-router with only selected controllers enabled.

claims.claim_text: The module sets a 100+ node threshold and makes an unsourced maturity comparison against Calico and Cilium.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.anchor_text: Large clusters (100+ nodes): BGP full mesh does not scale. You can use route reflectors, but at that scale, Calico or Cilium have more mature BGP implementations with better tooling.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Larger clusters usually need route reflectors or another peering design, because full-mesh BGP grows poorly as node count rises.
claims.rationale: The specific threshold and comparative tooling/maturity claim are not directly supported by a single allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: kube-router supports the standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy API.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 4 / NetworkPolicy Enforcement; section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.rationale: The upstream README says kube-router supports networking.k8s.io/NetworkPolicy semantics.

claims.claim_text: Standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy is limited to L3/L4 IP- and port-level controls rather than L7 HTTP method/path policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes NetworkPolicy documentation defines the API around IP addresses, ports, and L3/L4 traffic control.

claims.claim_text: The module contrasts kube-router with Hubble-style observability and richer eBPF tooling.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.anchor_text: You need eBPF-level observability: Kube-router has no equivalent to Hubble. If you need per-flow visibility, packet drop reasons, and DNS-aware policies, Cilium is the answer.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you need built-in eBPF-based flow visibility and richer troubleshooting features, kube-router does not provide a Hubble-style observability layer.
claims.rationale: The original wording mixes a supportable product difference with an absolute recommendation that is not citation-shaped.

claims.claim_text: Managed Kubernetes platforms ship with provider-specific networking integrations, and replacing the default stack can remove cloud-native features.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6 / The Sweet Spot
claims.anchor_text: Cloud-managed Kubernetes: EKS, GKE, and AKS come with their own CNI and service proxy integrations. Replacing them with kube-router would lose cloud-specific features (like security group integration, VPC routing).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Managed Kubernetes platforms ship with provider-specific networking integrations, so replacing the default stack can remove cloud-native features and increase operational work.
claims.rationale: The original sentence bundles three providers and specific feature examples without a single current primary source.

further_reading.title: kube-router Upstream Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for current kube-router architecture, feature scope, and project status.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Virtual IPs and Service Proxies
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains kube-proxy backends, including current IPVS behavior and its deprecation status in Kubernetes 1.35.

further_reading.title: RFC 4271: BGP-4
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical standards reference for BGP concepts discussed in the module.

notes: Per pipeline rules, fenced code blocks, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps were not decomposed into claim entries. The unsourced iptables-vs-IPVS benchmark table and the vendor comparison matrix still contain multiple cell-level factual claims and likely need a manual second pass if you want table-by-table citation coverage.
